skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through creating isolated Git worktrees for feature work, including directory selection, setup, and baseline checks.
skill.summary: Create isolated Git worktrees with safety checks before implementation work.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and filesystem patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are workflow instructions for Git worktrees, not hidden malware, but the setup steps can execute project dependency and test commands in a repository. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, credential access, or weak cryptography evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Project Setup Commands Can Execute Repository Code
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run package installation, build, and test commands such as npm install, cargo build, pip install, poetry install, and test runners. This is expected for a development workflow, but it can execute scripts from an untrusted repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly listed and are capable of executing project-controlled code. The intent appears legitimate because the skill is about preparing a development worktree.

medium_findings.title: Repository State Changes Are Encouraged
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the assistant to add a worktree directory to .gitignore and commit the change before proceeding. This can be correct, but it creates repository history changes that should be reviewed by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly require a file edit and commit. The risk is moderate because this is transparent workflow guidance, not covert modification.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Paths Are Benign Worktree Locations
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged .worktrees and ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees as hidden filesystem access. These paths are presented as workspace locations, not as attempts to read secrets or persist malware.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text describes selecting a worktree directory. No evidence shows access to credential files, shell profiles, or other sensitive hidden files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography on the description and integration lines. Review found no hash function, encryption code, or cryptographic API in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose about when to use the skill and related workflow phases. No cryptographic operation is present.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: The reviewed SKILL.md content does not contain override instructions, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, or requests to skip security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full skill file was reviewed and its content is a consistent Git worktree workflow. No suspicious prompt injection phrasing was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on shell examples for ls, grep, git, package managers, and test runners. These commands are expected for Git worktree automation but require normal command review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are plainly visible and central to the skill. They are not obfuscated or network-oriented, so the danger is contextual rather than malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Variable Path Construction
dangerous_patterns.description: The example builds a worktree path from LOCATION, project, and BRANCH_NAME variables before running git worktree add. Quoted variables reduce shell injection risk, but unsafe branch names could still create confusing paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present and mostly quoted, which lowers injection concern. The remaining risk depends on how the assistant validates branch names in real use.

content.value_statement: Feature work can disrupt the current workspace when branches, dependencies, and tests overlap. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through safe Git worktree setup before implementation.
actual_capabilities: Chooses between .worktrees, worktrees, and a global worktree directory.
Checks CLAUDE.md for project-specific worktree directory guidance.
Creates a new Git worktree and branch for feature work.
Detects common project files and runs setup commands.
Runs baseline tests and reports whether the workspace is ready.
limitations: Requires a Git repository that supports worktrees.
May run dependency installation and test commands defined by the project.
Does not resolve failing baseline tests without user direction.
Does not replace code review for generated commits or branch changes.

use_cases.title: Start Feature Work
use_cases.description: Create a separate workspace before implementing a new feature branch.

use_cases.title: Protect Current Changes
use_cases.description: Keep ongoing edits untouched while an assistant works in another branch.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Setup
use_cases.description: Give AI coding assistants a repeatable checklist for worktree creation and validation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create an isolated worktree for feature/login.

prompt_templates.title: Respect Project Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a worktree for this task. Check existing directories and CLAUDE.md before choosing the location.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Before Coding
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for the approved plan, install dependencies if needed, and run the baseline tests before editing code.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Setup Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an isolated worktree, report any install or test failures, and wait for my approval before continuing if the baseline fails.

output_examples.output: Worktree ready at the selected project-local directory. Baseline setup completed and tests passed.

output_examples.input: Start implementation, but keep my current workspace untouched.
output_examples.output: A new branch was prepared in an isolated worktree. Current workspace changes were not modified.

output_examples.input: Set up a worktree and run the baseline tests.
output_examples.output: The worktree was created, dependencies were checked, and baseline test results were reported before coding began.

best_practices: Review the selected worktree path before running setup in sensitive repositories.
Use project-local worktree directories only when they are ignored by Git.
Treat failing baseline tests as pre-existing issues until investigated.
anti_patterns: Creating a worktree in a tracked directory.
Running dependency installation in an untrusted repository without review.
Continuing implementation after baseline tests fail without user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It guides creation of a Git worktree with a new branch for isolated development.

faq.question: Does it modify my repository?
faq.answer: It may add a .gitignore entry and create a branch when the workflow requires it.

faq.question: Why does it check .gitignore?
faq.answer: The check helps prevent project-local worktree contents from being committed accidentally.

faq.question: Can it use a global worktree folder?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use a global path when no project-local directory is selected.

faq.question: Does it run tests automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow asks the assistant to run project-appropriate baseline tests.

faq.question: Is this safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Dependency installation and test commands can execute code from the repository.
